JavaScript OptimizationWhy Modern JavaScript Runs So Much Faster Than It Used To
← Part 2: The JavaScript Runtime
In Part 2, we looked at the JavaScript Runtime — the engine plus everything around it that lets JavaScript actually do useful things. In this part, we’re zooming back into the engine itself, to answer a question that trips up a lot of people: JavaScript is a high-level, dynamically typed language, so why does it run as fast as it does on modern websites and apps?

Introduction
Modern JavaScript engines are designed to run code as fast as possible.
When JavaScript was first created, performance was nowhere near as good as it is today. Over the years, browser developers have added many optimization techniques to make JavaScript applications faster and more efficient.
These optimizations happen automatically inside the JavaScript engine while your code is running — you don’t write anything special to trigger them, and most developers go their whole career without ever needing to think about them directly. But understanding what’s happening under the hood explains a lot about why some code patterns run faster than others.
In this part, we’ll look at some of the most important optimization techniques used by modern JavaScript engines.

What is Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation?
One of the biggest reasons modern JavaScript is fast is a technique called Just-In-Time Compilation, commonly known as JIT Compilation.
To understand JIT, we first need to understand two older, more traditional approaches that came before it:

• Compilation

• Interpretation
JIT, as we’ll see, isn’t really a brand-new third idea — it’s a clever combination of these two older ones, built specifically to get the best of both.

What is Compilation?
Concept: Compilation is the process of converting the entire source code into machine code before the program starts running.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

CompilationHow Compilation Works
Source Code
↓
Compilation
↓
Machine Code (Binary File)
↓
ExecutionThe compiler reads the entire program at once. After converting the program into machine code, it creates a binary file that the computer can execute. Only after compilation is complete does the program actually start running. Languages like C and C++ traditionally work this way — you compile the whole thing first, and only then do you get a runnable program.
Real-Life Example
Imagine a teacher gives you a 100-page book to translate. With compilation:

• Translate all 100 pages.

• Create the final translated book.

• Only then can someone start reading it.
Everything is prepared before execution begins — there’s no reading until the entire translation is finished, no matter how long that takes.
Advantages of Compilation

• Faster execution

• Better overall performance

• Machine code is fully ready before execution starts, so there’s no translation overhead while the program runs
Disadvantages of Compilation

• Initial compilation takes time

• The program cannot run at all until compilation finishes, even if you only need the first page

What is Interpretation?
Concept: Interpretation is a different approach entirely.
Instead of converting the entire program first, the interpreter reads and executes the code line by line.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

InterpretationHow Interpretation Works
Source Code
↓
Interpret and Execute
↓
OutputThe interpreter:

• Reads one line.

• Executes it.

• Moves to the next line.

• Executes it.

• Continues until the program ends.
Real-Life Example
Using the same book example, instead of translating all 100 pages first:

• Translate page 1.

• Read page 1.

• Translate page 2.

• Read page 2.
The work happens continuously, page by page, rather than all at once upfront.
Advantages of Interpretation

• Starts running almost immediately

• No need to wait for a full compilation pass before anything happens
Disadvantages of Interpretation

• Generally slower overall

• The same piece of code may need to be re-translated every single time it runs, since nothing gets saved in a precompiled form

Was JavaScript Originally Interpreted?
Yes.
In the early days, JavaScript was considered a purely interpreted language. The engine would read and execute code as it went, line by line, exactly like the book-translation example above.
However, interpreted languages are usually slower than compiled languages, for exactly the reason listed above — repeated re-translation of the same code adds up. As web applications became larger and more complex through the 2000s and 2010s, browsers needed a faster solution, because a sluggish interpreter couldn’t keep up with the increasingly app-like websites people were building.
This need is exactly what led to the development of Just-In-Time Compilation.

What is Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilation?
Concept: Just-In-Time Compilation combines the advantages of both compilation and interpretation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Just-In-Time CompilationHow JIT Works
Source Code
↓
Compilation
↓
Machine Code
↓
Immediate ExecutionUnlike traditional compilation:

• Machine code is generated, but

• No separate binary file is created and saved to disk

• Execution starts immediately after compilation, rather than waiting on a full build step
This makes JavaScript dramatically faster than pure interpretation, while still keeping the “start running right away” feel that interpreted languages are known for. If you remember from Part 1, this is exactly what V8’s two-part system of Ignition and TurboFan is built to do — Ignition gets code running fast via a lightweight bytecode pass, and TurboFan steps in afterward to compile the frequently used parts into genuinely fast machine code. JIT is the general concept; Ignition and TurboFan are V8’s specific implementation of it.

Why is JIT Important?
Without JIT:

• JavaScript applications would be noticeably slower

• Modern web applications would feel less responsive, with visible lag on common interactions

• Complex websites would take longer to execute the code behind animations, scrolling, and dynamic content
JIT allows JavaScript to achieve performance that gets surprisingly close to traditionally compiled languages, despite JavaScript being dynamically typed and far more flexible at runtime than something like C++.

How Modern JavaScript Engines Optimize Code
Modern engines don’t stop after the first compilation pass. They continue improving the code while it is running. The process generally looks like this:
Source Code
↓
Parse
↓
Compile
↓
Generate Unoptimized Machine Code
↓
Start Execution Immediately
↓
Analyze Code Usage
↓
Recompile and Optimize
↓
Generate Better Machine CodeThis optimization may happen multiple times during execution, not just once. The engine keeps improving the code as it learns which parts are used most frequently — the longer your program runs, the more chances the engine has had to make it faster.

Why Create Unoptimized Code First?
You might wonder: why not generate the best possible machine code immediately, right from the start?
Because that would delay execution. Generating highly optimized machine code takes real analysis time, and making the user wait for that analysis before anything runs at all would defeat the whole purpose. Instead:

• Generate a basic, “good enough” version quickly.

• Start execution immediately.

• Improve the code in the background while it’s already running.
This gives users a faster experience overall — a page that starts responding in milliseconds and then quietly gets faster as it goes, rather than a page that makes you wait upfront for a perfect version.

Background Optimization
While your program is running:

• The engine monitors function calls

• It tracks frequently used code (often called “hot” code)

• It performs optimization in the background, without pausing your program to do so

• It recompiles important sections into faster machine code
All of this happens automatically, with zero input from you as a developer. Most developers never even notice it’s happening — your code just gets faster over time as it runs, almost like the engine is quietly learning your program’s habits.

Special Engine Threads
The processes of:

• Parsing

• Compilation

• Optimization
happen inside special threads of the JavaScript engine, separate from the single thread your actual JavaScript code runs on. These internal engine threads are not directly accessible from JavaScript code — you can’t write JS that pokes at them or controls them. The engine handles all of this entirely behind the scenes, which is part of why JavaScript can stay single-threaded from a developer’s point of view while still doing genuinely multi-threaded work internally to keep things fast.

What is Inlining?
Concept: Inlining is another optimization technique used by JavaScript engines.
The goal is simple: reduce unnecessary function calls.
Example
function add(a, b) {
return a + b;
}let result = 0;result = result + 5;
result = result + 3;console.log(result);Normally, when a function is called:
add(5, 3);JavaScript has to:

• Create a function call

• Push it onto the call stack

• Execute the function

• Return the result
This process takes time — small amounts of time per call, but it adds up fast if a function is called thousands or millions of times, like inside a loop.

What Does Inlining Do?
If the engine notices a function is called many times, it may replace the function call with the actual code inside the function, directly at the call site.
Instead of:
add(5, 3);the engine internally treats it like:
5 + 3;This removes the overhead of repeated function calls entirely — there’s no longer a call to set up, push onto the stack, or return from. It’s worth knowing you can’t force this yourself; the engine decides when inlining makes sense based on how your code actually behaves while running, which is part of why writing small, simple, frequently-called functions is often just as fast as writing everything inline by hand.
Benefits of Inlining

• Faster execution

• Fewer function calls

• Reduced call stack operations

• Better overall performance

Performance Considerations
Even though JavaScript engines are highly optimized, developers should still write efficient code. Good coding practices can significantly improve performance, even with all this automatic optimization working in the background. Two common recommendations are:

• Minimize DOM Manipulation

• Reduce Unnecessary Function Calls

Minimize DOM Manipulation
DOM operations are relatively expensive — far more expensive than regular JavaScript operations like math or string handling. Every time JavaScript modifies the DOM, the browser may need to:

• Recalculate layout

• Repaint elements

• Update the screen
Frequent DOM changes can slow down web pages noticeably, especially on lower-powered devices.
Better Approach
Instead of updating the DOM repeatedly:
element.innerHTML += "A";
element.innerHTML += "B";
element.innerHTML += "C";Batch updates whenever possible — build the full string first, then update the DOM once:
element.innerHTML = "A" + "B" + "C";This reduces browser workload significantly, since the browser only has to recalculate layout and repaint once instead of three separate times.

Reduce Function Calls
Every function call creates a small amount of overhead. Too many unnecessary function calls can affect performance, particularly inside loops that run many times.
Inefficient Code
function calculateTotal(a, b, c) {
return addNumbers(a, b) + multiplyNumbers(c, b);
}
function addNumbers(x, y) {
return x + y;
}
function multiplyNumbers(x, y) {
return x * y;
}Here, calculateTotal() calls two separate functions. Each function call requires additional work from the engine — creating a call, pushing it onto the stack, and returning from it.
Improved Version
function calculateTotal(a, b,
